text.title: MoSCoW Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common failure modes that invalidate MoSCoW results, with root causes and fixes.

antipattern.description: Must inflation: 60-80% of items end up categorized as Must Have.
antipattern.reason: The fail-test is applied loosely. Teams interpret "important" as "must." Without a contrarian pass, no item survives downgrade to Should.

antipattern.description: No Won't Have category. Teams skip documenting exclusions because it is uncomfortable.
antipattern.reason: Won't Have is the primary mechanism for preventing scope creep. An empty Won't list means every excluded item is implicitly negotiable, re-entering as "just one more thing."

antipattern.description: No timebox declared. MoSCoW session runs without a stated capacity number.
antipattern.reason: Without capacity as a constraint, categories reflect desires rather than trade-offs. The 60/20/20 allocation cannot be validated, so the output is a wishlist, not a scoped plan.

antipattern.description: Treating the 60/20/20 allocation as a law rather than a heuristic, and using it to mechanically assign exact effort budgets.
antipattern.reason: The allocation is a directional check. Forcing exactly 60% Must effort produces false precision. The check is: "are Must+Should within 80%?" not "is Must exactly 60%?"

antipattern.description: Changing Must Have items mid-sprint without escalation. PMs add scope by re-categorizing Could items as Must.
antipattern.reason: Must changes after lockdown destroy the entire value of the exercise. Every Must change must require explicit stakeholder escalation and re-validation of capacity allocation.

antipattern.description: Using MoSCoW for long-horizon roadmaps (more than one quarter).
antipattern.reason: MoSCoW categories are release-scoped. Over a quarter, business context shifts enough that Must items become irrelevant and Won't items become Must. Rerun per release, not per quarter.
